---
title: "1.1.6 Two's complement (binary numbers)"
subtitle: "Data Representation P22-P24"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        <style>
        .reveal .term { color: #cf1f2e; font-weight: 700; }
        .reveal .mark { background: #fff36d; padding: 0 0.08em; }
        .reveal p { margin-top: 0.4em; margin-bottom: 0.25em; }
        .reveal .tight-list li { margin-bottom: 0.14em; }
        .reveal .clean-table table { font-size: 0.78em; line-height: 1.15; }
        .reveal .clean-table th, .reveal .clean-table td { padding: 0.22em 0.30em; vertical-align: top; text-align: center; }
        </style>
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 1 Directory / 返回 Unit 1 目录](../../index.html)

## Two's Complement

### Representing positive and negative integers

Up until now, we have assumed all binary numbers are positive integers. To allow the possibility of representing <span class="mark">negative integers</span> we make use of <span class="term">two's complement</span> (二进制补码). In this section we will again assume <span class="mark">8-bit registers</span> are being used.

Only one minor change to the binary headings needs to be introduced here:

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c}
\mathbf{-128} & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1
\end{array}$$

::: {.tight-list}
- the left-most bit is changed to a <span class="mark">negative value</span>
- the new range of possible numbers is <span class="mark">$-128$ ($10000000_2$) to $+127$ ($01111111_2$)</span>
- a <span class="mark">$1$-value in the left-most bit</span> indicates a negative number and a <span class="mark">$0$-value</span> indicates a positive number
:::

## Writing Positive Binary Numbers

### Positive numbers in two's complement format

The following two examples show how we can write the following positive binary numbers in the two's complement format: <span class="mark">$19$</span> and <span class="mark">$4$</span>

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c}
-128 & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1
\end{array}$$

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c}
-128 & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}$$

As you will notice, for positive binary numbers, it is no different to what was done in Section 1.1.2.

## Converting Positive Denary Numbers

### Positive denary to two's complement format

If we wish to convert a positive denary number to the two's complement format, we do exactly the same as in Section 1.1.2:

::: {.tight-list}
- $38$ gives <span class="mark">`00100110`</span>
- $125$ gives <span class="mark">`01111101`</span>
:::

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c}
-128 & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0
\end{array}$$

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c}
-128 & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
0 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1
\end{array}$$

## Converting Positive Binary Numbers

### Positive two's complement to denary

Each time a $1$ appears in a column, the column value is added to the total:

::: {.tight-list}
- `01101110` gives <span class="mark">$64 + 32 + 8 + 4 + 2 = 110$</span>
- `00111111` gives <span class="mark">$32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 63$</span>
:::

## Reading Negative Two's Complement

### Interpreting numbers with MSB $= 1$

Each time a $1$ appears in a column, the column value is added to the total:

**Example:** `10010011`

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c}
-128 & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1
\end{array}$$

$-128 + 16 + 2 + 1 = \mathbf{-109}$

**Example:** `11100100`

$-128 + 64 + 32 + 4 = \mathbf{-28}$

**Example:** `11110101`

$-128 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 4 + 1 = \mathbf{-11}$

A two's complement number with a <span class="mark">$1$-value in the $-128$ column</span> must represent a negative binary number.

## Converting Negative Denary Numbers

### Method 1 and Method 2

Consider the number <span class="mark">$+67$</span> in 8-bit two's complement binary format:

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c}
-128 & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1
\end{array}$$

**Method 1**: put $1$s in their correct places for <span class="mark">$-67$</span>

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c}
-128 & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1
\end{array}$$

since <span class="mark">$-128 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 4 + 1 = -67$</span>

**Method 2**: first write the number as a positive binary value, then invert and add $1$

```text
 67  = 01000011
invert -> 10111100
+1     -> 10111101
```

## Worked Example: $-79$

### Convert $-79$ into 8-bit two's complement

**Method 1**

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c}
-128 & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1
\end{array}$$

because <span class="mark">$-79 = -128 + 49$</span> and $49 = 32 + 16 + 1$.

**Method 2**

```text
 79     = 01001111
invert  = 10110000
+1      = 10110001
```

Both methods give <span class="mark">`10110001`</span>. It is a good idea to practise both methods.

## Not Limited to 8 Bits

### Two's complement with fewer bits

When applying two's complement, it isn't always necessary for a binary number to have $8$ bits:

$$\begin{array}{c c c c}
-8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
0 & 1 & 1 & 0
\end{array}$$

Applying two's complement (`1001 + 1`) would give:

$$\begin{array}{c c c c}
-8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 1 & 0
\end{array}$$

In other words: <span class="mark">$-6$</span>

## Example with 12 Bits

### Converting $-1676$

The following <span class="mark">$12$-bit</span> binary number represents denary number <span class="mark">$1676$</span>:

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c c c c c}
-2048 & 1024 & 512 & 256 & 128 & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}$$

Applying two's complement (`100101110011 + 1`) would give:

$$\begin{array}{c c c c c c c c c c c c}
-2048 & 1024 & 512 & 256 & 128 & 64 & 32 & 16 & 8 & 4 & 2 & 1 \\
1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0
\end{array}$$

In other words: <span class="mark">$-1676$</span>

## Classroom Check

### Understand two's complement representation

A complete answer should include:

::: {.tight-list}
- that <span class="term">two's complement</span> allows representation of <span class="mark">both positive and negative integers</span> in binary
- that the left-most heading changes from <span class="mark">$+128$ to $-128$</span> in an 8-bit register
- that a <span class="mark">$0$-value</span> in the left-most bit indicates a positive number and a <span class="mark">$1$-value</span> indicates a negative number
- that positive numbers are written in the <span class="mark">same way</span> as ordinary binary, provided the left-most bit is $0$
- the two methods for negative numbers: <span class="mark">direct placement using the negative left-most column</span>, or <span class="mark">invert all bits and add $1$</span>
- the 8-bit two's complement range: <span class="mark">$-128$ to $+127$</span>
:::

## Bridge

### Next: Character Sets (ASCII and Unicode)

The next deck covers how computers represent text.

## End

### Return to the main outline

[← Back to Unit 1 Directory / 返回 Unit 1 目录](../../index.html)
